text: The XI robotic port placement is shown with four 8mm ports in a horizontal line above the umbilicus, and a green 12mm assistant port triangulated inferiorly. A high epigastric trocar is used for the Nathanson liver retractor. The camera and instruments are positioned, revealing the pre-pyloric area and distal antrum, with the rest of the stomach in the chest. The surgeon incises the peritoneum at the anterior arch of the crura to access the mediastinal plane anterior to the hernia sac.

The surgeon uses a mini laparotomy pad for rapid blunt dissection and a harmonic scalpel for energy dissection. The hepatic neurovascular bundle is preserved to prevent liver devascularization and maintain vagal branches to the gallbladder. The right and left cruciate of the diaphragm are exposed anteriorly, and the esophagus is visible in the mediastinum.

The surgeon begins to visualize the left vagus nerve arching away from the esophagus, tented within areolar tissue attached to the retracted hernia sac. The nerve is mobilized to remain with the esophagus. Inferiorly, the end of the vagus nerve is visible where it connects to the stomach. The surgeon divides the anterior surface of the large hiatal hernia sac vertically, aiding in exposure. Using a vessel sealer device, the surgeon removes the final lateral attachments of the hernia sac to the pleura, revealing the intact pleura with the lung behind it in the mediastinum.

In the abdominal cavity, the surgeon has successfully brought down the entire stomach along with the large hernia sac. The hernia sac is being divided away from the stomach using a vessel sealer device and a harmonic scalpel to cut the short gastric vessels, preparing for a floppy Nissen fundoplication.

The surgeon uses the newly released vessel sealer extend, which is slimmer and faster, to dissect and retract tissues. The instrument is used to sew and tie knots. The short gastric vessels are being divided around the top of the fundus. The surgeon approaches the lesser omentum at the pars flaccida, exposing the right cruce of the diaphragm and separating it from the perigastric tissues. The vena cava is visible in the background.

The surgeon creates a large window posterior to the stomach and esophagus, revealing both crura of the diaphragm inferiorly. The posterior vagus nerve is visible and is carefully lifted to remain with the esophagus. A dissection window is formed between the nerve and the aorta, continuing the circumferential mobilization of the lower esophagus, with the aorta visible just posterior to the dissection site.

The surgeon takes down the final posterior attachments tethering the esophagus in the mediastinum. A soft Penrose drain loop is passed posterior to the esophagus, just superior to the gastroesophageal junction, and the ends are held together with an end-of-loop device for atraumatic retraction. The next step involves closure of the crura.

The surgeon uses an O-ethabon suture in a figure of eight pattern to cinch the large curl defect together, reducing intra-abdominal pressure to ease tension on the closure line. The posterior closure is performed behind the esophagus, avoiding the hepatic neurovascular bundle with articulated robotic instruments. SH needles are used to take bites of the peritoneum for buttressing, with plans to reinforce the closure with mesh due to the large hiatal opening.

The surgeon changes the direction of the crura closure from vertical to a hockey stick configuration, moving horizontally to reduce tension and minimize acute angulation of the esophagus as it passes through the diaphragm. A bougie sizing device is visible through the esophagus into the stomach during this closure. The surgeon measures for mesh placement, using a seven by ten centimeter Phasix ST mesh with an intestinal barrier coating, secured with absorbable Vicryl sutures. The mesh is tucked behind the left and caudate lobes of the liver for stabilization.

The surgeon ensures the bulk of the mesh overlaps the primary crural closure, removing fluid and tucking the posterior portion of the mesh behind the spleen, ensuring no fat is between the mesh and diaphragm. A marking suture is placed well posterior on the fundus, behind the greater curvature line, to ensure a symmetrical wrap. The fundus is passed behind the window, posterior to the esophagus, performing the shoeshine maneuver to ensure a loose, tension-free wrap. Permanent sutures are used to secure the wrap.

The surgeon sutures the right side of the fundus, part of the esophagus, and the left side of the fundus, ensuring the vagus nerve is not entrapped. A third suture is placed between the two edges of the fundus, creating a short, two-centimeter floppy wrap. The wrap is then pexed to the undersurface of the diaphragm at multiple points.

The surgeon removes the Penrose retracting loop and the sizing bougie. A portion of the greater curvature of the stomach is fixed to the anterior abdominal wall using a strong figure of eight suture, completing the robotic-assisted laparoscopic repair of a paraesophageal hiatal hernia.
